steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The administrators claim their survey data shows that concerns about virtual tours discouraging in-person visits were unjustified. A strong answer must show that virtual tours did not deter in-person visitors — ideally that the tours actively encouraged them.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Most surveyed visitors to the Museo del Prado indicated that they lived somewhere other than Madrid.: Where visitors live has no bearing on whether virtual tours discouraged in-person attendance. This does not address the concern at all. ✗
  • Choice B — Many surveyed visitors to the Museo del Prado indicated that the virtual tours convinced them to plan an in-person visit.: This directly refutes the feared outcome. Instead of virtual tours making in-person visits seem unnecessary, they motivated people to come in person. This is exactly what the administrators need to show. ✓
  • Choice C — Many surveyed visitors to the Museo del Prado indicated that they would likely view the virtual tours in order to reminisce about their in-person visit.: Post-visit nostalgia use of virtual tours says nothing about whether pre-visit tours deterred attendance. The timing and direction of the effect are wrong. ✗
  • Choice D — Most surveyed visitors to the Museo del Prado indicated that they were unaware of the virtual tours before their first in-person visit.: Unawareness of virtual tours before visiting means those visitors were unaffected by them — but this does not show the tours were harmless for those who did see them, nor that they helped. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
